source.title: The ubiquity of model-based reinforcement learning

confidence_rationale: Review-level synthesis arguing that signatures of model-based learning are pervasive in the very same brain regions historically considered model-free territory (striatum, dopamine system). Cuts BOTH ways for MECH-312: it argues that the binary MB-vs-MF decomposition is too clean (which weakens the simple two-channel framing) but it also reaffirms that there ARE distinct computations being arbitrated (which supports the multi-channel framing). Mixed direction reflects this.
failure_signatures: An REE arbitration substrate that strictly partitions channels into anatomically separated regions will fail Doll 2012's empirical observation: the same regions show both MB and MF signatures depending on context. MECH-312 may need to be implemented as a soft mixing weight rather than hard channel selection.

If REE's ARC-062 and ARC-064 are designed to produce categorically distinct neural signatures, but biological evidence is that the same substrates host both, then the substrate-level distinguishability of REE's two pathways may be artificially crisp.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Signatures of model-based reinforcement-learning computations (e.g., outcome-based action evaluation that uses internal world models) are pervasive in brain regions historically considered the substrate of model-free learning, including the striatum and the dopamine system. The dichotomy between MB and MF systems may be more graded than originally proposed; the same neural substrates may host both computations under different operating regimes. The review attempts to reconcile this empirical 'cohabitation' with the dual-systems framework.
mapping.ree_translation: For MECH-312, this review supplies a cautionary tale about strict-partition substrate designs. If the biological dual-systems literature is moving away from anatomically-separated MB/MF channels toward shared-substrate-with-soft-arbitration, REE's MECH-312 should consider implementing arbitration as a soft mixing weight on shared substrates rather than as a hard switch between separated channels. This is also the reason for the mixed evidence direction -- the review weakens strict two-channel framings (good for justifying expansion to 3+ channels) but also reaffirms multi-computation arbitration is necessary (good for justifying MECH-312 at all).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Doll et al. is a Current Opinion review, not a primary empirical paper. Its claims summarise an emerging consensus rather than test it; subsequent empirical work has nuanced but not overturned the picture. The mapping to MECH-312's design is conceptual rather than direct: it cautions against over-engineering channel separation, but does not prescribe an implementation. Transfer risk moderate.
mapping.source_context: Curr Opin Neurobiol review by Doll, Simon and Daw. Cited >700 times. Functions as a state-of-the-field-2012 synthesis on the dual-systems framework.

confidence_components.notes: Curr Opin Neurobiol review venue (high quality, secondary literature). Mapping fidelity moderate -- the warning about strict-partition substrates is conceptually relevant but does not directly anchor a substrate-design choice for REE. The mixed evidence-direction reflects that the paper both supports and complicates the dual-systems framework.
